content:
11 min read
2 hours ago

--

Most React performance problems are not caused by rendering too often. They are caused by unclear state ownership, unstable identity, effect-driven synchronization, and components that cannot explain why they rendered.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most React performance problems do not start with too many renders.
They start with code that cannot clearly explain why it rendered.
A value lives in local state, Redux, context, URL params, and a cache key. A component receives new object references on every render. An effect updates state because another effect updated state. A memo is added, then another memo is added to protect the first memo.
The app looks like it has a re-render problem.
But the real problem is that nobody owns the truth clearly enough.
That is why many React performance fixes feel strange. A developer sees too many renders, adds useMemo, wraps a component with React.memo, moves a function into useCallback, and maybe splits a component. The profiler looks better in one place. Then another screen slows down. Another dependency changes. Another object reference breaks memoization. Another effect runs again.
The render was visible.
The architecture problem was underneath.
React re-renders are not automatically bad. Rendering is how React keeps the UI aligned with state. The real issue is not that React rendered. The issue is whether the render had a clear reason, a clear owner, and a predictable cost.
If the answer is no, fewer renders will not save the codebase.
They will only hide the confusion for a little longer.

The Render Is Usually the Symptom, Not the Cause
React developers often notice performance problems at the render layer because that is where the pain becomes visible.
A list feels slow. A dashboard stutters. A form loses responsiveness. A table rerenders when a small filter changes. A modal becomes laggy after adding one more component. The immediate reaction is to ask, “Why is this rendering so much?”
That is a useful question.
It is not always the first question.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A render is often the final visible result of earlier design decisions. State changed somewhere. A context value changed. A parent created a new object. A selector returned a new reference. A query refetched. An effect set state. A prop was derived in an unstable way. The component rendered because something upstream told it to.
If you only stare at the component that rendered, you may miss the source of movement.
This is how teams end up optimizing symptoms. They wrap the slow child in React.memo, but the parent keeps passing a new config object. They memoize the object, but one dependency changes every render. They stabilize the dependency, but an effect updates state after every fetch. They patch the effect, but the real issue is that the same filter state exists in three places.
The bug did not start in the render.
It arrived there.
This matters because React performance is not only about reducing work. It is about understanding why work happens. A render with a clear reason is easier to optimize. A render caused by hidden ownership, unstable references, and synchronization effects is harder because the code does not explain itself.
A strong React codebase makes render causes visible.
A weak one makes developers chase movement after it has already spread.
State Ownership Is Where Performance Starts Breaking
Most React performance problems become confusing when state ownership is unclear.
A value can live in many places: local component state, parent state, context, Redux, server cache, URL params, form state, local storage, or derived render values. None of those places is wrong by default. The mistake is letting multiple places own the same meaning without deciding why.
A filter is a common example.
The URL has ?status=active. A component keeps status in local state. A query key uses a separate filter object. A table stores its own internal filter. An export button builds filters again from selected UI values. Then an effect tries to keep everything synchronized.
The UI may work.
But every change becomes expensive because the app has several versions of the truth.
When the filter changes, which value updates first? Does the URL drive the cache, or does local state drive the URL? Should pagination reset when the URL changes? Should export use the current page, all filtered rows, or the last successful query? Should the table show stale data while fetching, or clear immediately?
These are not re-render questions.
They are ownership questions.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

When ownership is unclear, React renders become noisy. One state update triggers another. One effect reacts to a derived value. One cache update causes a context update. One local reset causes a parent rerender. The app starts moving in circles because no layer clearly owns the truth.
A better React design starts by deciding what kind of state the value is. Server state should usually be owned by the server cache or data layer. URL state should represent shareable navigation state. Local UI state should represent temporary interface behavior. Form state should own drafts. Derived values should usually be calculated from existing state instead of stored again.
This does not remove all renders.
It removes pointless uncertainty.
React feels faster when the code knows where truth lives.
Unstable Identity Makes React Look Busier Than It Is
Sometimes React rerenders because values keep pretending to be new.
A parent creates a new object during every render. A callback is recreated and passed to a memoized child. An array is filtered inline and sent as a prop. A context provider creates a new value object every time anything changes. A selector returns a new reference even when the meaningful data is the same.
React sees new identity.
The developer sees unnecessary render.
This is where many teams reach for memoization. That can be correct. But memoization is only useful when you understand which identity should remain stable and why.
The weak fix is adding useMemo everywhere because something rerendered.
The stronger fix is asking what identity means in this part of the app.
For example, a table receiving columns should not get a new columns array on every render if the columns are conceptually stable. A child component receiving filters should not receive a freshly created object unless the filters actually changed. A context provider should not force every consumer to update because one unrelated field changed inside a large shared value.
Identity instability creates performance bugs because it breaks React’s ability to skip work.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

But the deeper problem is often careless boundaries. A component is passing too much. A context owns too much. A parent derives too many objects inline. A child depends on broad props instead of specific values. The tree structure makes every small change flow through components that do not care about it.
Memoization can help stabilize identity.
It cannot fix unclear data flow by itself.
If a component receives a giant settings object but only needs theme, memoizing the object is less helpful than passing the specific value. If a context contains user, permissions, notifications, flags, and layout state, memoizing the provider value may still cause broad updates because too many unrelated meanings share one channel.
Stable identity is not only a hook problem.
It is a design problem about what changes together.
Effects Make Renders Worse When They Repair Unclear Flow
Some of the worst render problems come from effects that exist only to patch state flow.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A prop changes, so an effect copies it into local state. Local state changes, so another effect updates the URL. The URL changes, so another effect updates filters. Filters change, so another effect resets pagination. Pagination changes, so another effect triggers a fetch.
Nothing here is obviously wrong in isolation.
Together, it becomes a render machine.
The component no longer reads like a clear flow of state. It reads like a set of reactions trying to keep each other honest. One state change creates another state change. One effect runs because another effect wrote something. The component renders not because the UI meaning changed clearly, but because the code is negotiating with itself.
A small example shows the pattern:
useEffect(() => {
setFilters(parseFiltersFromUrl(searchParams));
}, [searchParams]);useEffect(() => {
setSearchParams(buildUrlFromFilters(filters));
}, [filters]);
This can be made to work with guards, comparisons, and careful dependencies.
But the deeper question is: who owns the filters?
If the URL owns them, derive filters from the URL and avoid storing a second truth. If local state owns a draft before applying filters, name it as a draft and only write to the URL on apply. If the server query owns the active filters, make the query key reflect that directly.
Effects are not bad.
Effects become painful when they compensate for a missing state model.
This is why dependency arrays feel impossible in messy components. The array is not the root problem. It is exposing how many values the effect depends on because the effect is doing too many jobs. Fetching, syncing, resetting, tracking, and deriving may all be tangled in one place.
A good effect has a narrow reason to exist.
A suspicious effect exists because the code never decided where a piece of truth belongs.
Memoization Helps Only After the Model Is Clear
useMemo, useCallback, and React.memo are useful tools.
They are not architecture.
This matters because many React performance fixes begin at the wrong level. A developer sees renders, adds memoization, and expects the system to feel cleaner. Sometimes it does. Often, it only adds another layer of conditions around a model that is still unclear.
Memoization works best when the code already has stable meaning.
If a calculation is expensive and depends on specific inputs, memoize it. If a callback is passed to a memoized child and should only change when its real dependencies change, stabilize it. If a component receives stable props and is expensive to render, React.memo can help. These are practical tools.
But memoization becomes frustrating when it is used to hide unstable ownership.
A memo is added to protect a child from a parent that owns too much. A callback is stabilized, but it closes over state that should not live there. A derived object is memoized, but its dependencies change constantly because the upstream data shape is unstable. A component is wrapped in React.memo, but it still rerenders because context updates every time.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The team keeps adding optimization layers.
The actual data model remains messy.
This is why memoization often feels fragile. It depends on clear dependencies. If the dependencies are unclear, the memo becomes another place where developers guess. Include too much, and the memo recalculates constantly. Include too little, and the UI becomes stale. Add a linter override, and future changes become risky.
The right order matters.
First, decide ownership. Then reduce unnecessary state. Then stabilize identities that should be stable. Then memoize expensive work where it has a clear benefit. Then measure whether the change actually helped.
Memoization is powerful when it protects a good design.
It is weak when it disguises a confused one.
Expensive Components Need Boundaries, Not Random Optimizations
Some React components are naturally expensive.
Large tables. Complex dashboards. Rich editors. Charts. Search pages. Forms with conditional validation. Drag-and-drop interfaces. Permission-heavy admin screens. These areas need performance attention because their render cost is real.
But even there, random optimization is not enou
